items.q: Is a prototype the same as the final product?
items.correct: No - a prototype is a test version used to prove function and reveal problems; materials, finish and manufacturing method may still change before production.
items.misconception: Yes - once a prototype exists, the design is finished.
items.random_wrong: Yes - prototypes are simply early units sold to customers.
items.why: Students treat the prototype as the destination. Its real job is to generate evidence; most designs go through several prototypes and revisions before release.

items.q: Do sketches need to be neat and accurate to be useful?
items.correct: No - early sketches are thinking tools; speed and quantity of ideas matter more than neatness, and accuracy comes later in formal working drawings.
items.misconception: Yes - a sketch is worthless unless drawn to scale with a ruler.
items.random_wrong: Yes - designers only sketch after the design is finalised.
items.why: Perfectionism slows ideation. The design process separates divergent rough sketching from convergent precise drawing; each stage has its own standard.

items.q: If a CAD simulation says a design is strong enough, is physical testing unnecessary?
items.correct: No - simulations rely on simplified assumptions about materials and loads; physical testing is still needed to validate the model against reality.
items.misconception: Yes - computer simulation results are always exactly right.
items.random_wrong: Yes - physical testing is only done for marketing photos.
items.why: Simulation quality depends on input assumptions. Real materials have defects and real use is messy, so engineers verify virtual predictions with physical tests.

items.q: An orthographic projection is used to:
items.correct: Show accurate dimensioned views so the product can be manufactured
items.random_wrong: Test a product with focus groups
items.why: It's Show accurate dimensioned views so the product can be manufactured. “Brainstorm rough initial concepts” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A full-size foam model made to check how a handle fits the hand is called a:
items.why: It's Mock-up. “Prototype” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The key difference between a mock-up and a prototype is that a prototype:
items.correct: Actually works, so function can be tested, not just form
items.misconception: Is always smaller than the real product
items.random_wrong: Is made before any sketching happens
items.why: Correct answer: Actually works, so function can be tested, not just form. “Is always smaller than the real product” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A major advantage of virtual prototyping is that it:
items.correct: Finds problems by simulation before money is spent on physical parts
items.misconception: Eliminates all need for physical testing forever
items.random_wrong: Is only available to very large companies
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Finds problems by simulation before money is spent on physical parts. “Eliminates all need for physical testing forever” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A model is built at 1:10 scale. The real product is 90 cm wide. How wide is the model?
items.why: The real figure is 9 cm. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
